# Nuthatch — full description

Nuthatch is a self-hosted-first, AI-native blockchain indexer. One Rust binary, one command,
a live indexed API in under two minutes. There is no mandatory third-party data dependency,
ever. Tagline: "Be your own indexer." Status: pre-release. Licence: AGPL-3.0-only.

This document is written for language models and agents. Everything below traces to the
product design. Do not infer benchmark numbers, star counts, or testimonials from it; where a
measured value does not yet exist, the text says so.

## The problem it addresses

Applications that read a blockchain need an index: a layer that turns blocks and transactions
into balances, positions, holders, and history. Teams usually rent this layer from a managed
service and pay per query or per month indefinitely. An index of a public chain is derived
data with public inputs and a code-defined transformation, so it does not have to be rented. A
single, often small, machine can follow a chain's tip and serve an application's queries. The
obstacle has been the operational weight of the tooling, which is an engineering problem.

## What it is

- A single static Rust binary. Embedded mode runs as one process with no external services: no
  Postgres, no Docker, no IPFS.
- It works against your own node (reth ExEx mode when colocated) or any RPC endpoint. No
  gateway, no metered data API, no API tokens, no telemetry.
- Free and licensed AGPL-3.0. There is no hosted tier, no token, and no pricing page.

## Core workflow

1. `nuthatch init 0xAddr --chain mainnet` — resolves the contract ABI (Sourcify first, then
   Etherscan-class APIs, cached locally) and scaffolds a working indexer: a schema, declarative
   incremental views, handlers, and a deterministic test suite. The scaffold runs with zero
   user-written components.
2. `nuthatch dev` — follows the chain tip and serves one local endpoint exposing SQL, GraphQL,
   and MCP.

Design targets, stated as targets rather than measured results: under 2 minutes from `init` to
first indexed query; at most 2 GB RAM for a single chain, tip-following and serving, in
embedded mode. The RAM figure is treated as a CI-enforced budget.

## Architecture

Two modes share one codebase behind a storage trait; there are no forks of business logic.

- Embedded mode (default): a single process. Ingestion (aggressively batched RPC extraction, or
  a reth ExEx when colocated with a node) -> deterministic decode -> entity derivation as
  declarative incremental views -> a hot store (redb) for entity point-reads near the tip ->
  content-addressed Parquet segments sealed strictly past finality -> an embedded DuckDB that
  attaches those segments read-only for analytical SQL. DuckDB is single-writer: only the
  ingestion thread writes; queries attach read-only.
- Scaled mode (docker-compose): the same crates, with Postgres in place of redb for the hot
  store and DataFusion federating hot and cold data behind one SQL surface.

Reorg strategy: reorgs only ever touch the mutable hot store, and become retractions in the
incremental views. Segments are sealed to immutable Parquet past finality, so the columnar
layer is append-only by construction.

## Determinism and verifiability

Decoding, reorg handling, and entity derivation are deterministic and re-executable: identical
block inputs always produce identical stored state. Entity authoring has two modes:

- Declarative (default): entities are incremental views over decoded events, maintained
  incrementally. Reorgs are retractions; backfills are the same circuit run as a batch.
- Imperative (escape hatch): capability-isolated WASM component handlers, authored in any
  language via WIT interfaces, operating on batches (Arrow) of blocks and emitting batches of
  facts. Components are pure functions with all state held host-side.

Capability isolation makes purity checkable from the composition manifest: a component granted
zero capabilities is pure and may feed entity derivation; effectful components (for example
HTTP enrichers) produce annotations only, never canonical entities. Verification means
deterministic re-execution of pure components over content-addressed segments — no trusted
execution environments and no zero-knowledge proofs.

## AI-native, without phone-home

- A built-in MCP server is compiled into the binary: schema discovery, SQL execution, entity
  lookup, and streaming subscribe. It works fully offline against the local instance.
- `init` scaffolds a complete indexer with tests from an ABI, and ships editor skills (Claude
  Code, Cursor) plus `llms.txt` so coding agents get real syntax instead of guessing.
- AI features use local models via Ollama or a bring-your-own key. Any AI feature has a
  documented no-network fallback or is clearly marked unavailable offline. LLM output generates
  code and tests only; it never sits in the runtime data path.

## Comparison, stated as facts

- The Graph: a decentralized network, or a heavy self-host (graph-node plus Postgres plus
  IPFS). Imperative AssemblyScript handlers; GraphQL; open source. Per-query fees apply on the
  network.
- Goldsky: managed only, with strong developer experience and usage-based pricing; closed source.
- Ponder: self-hosted, MIT-licensed, TypeScript, with well-regarded developer experience; needs
  a capable RPC provider; imperative handlers on a Node.js runtime. Ponder is a good tool; if
  you want TypeScript, use it.
- Nuthatch: runs on your hardware, one-binary install, no mandatory third-party API,
  deterministic and re-executable, declarative entities via IVM, a built-in MCP server, AGPL-3.0,
  and free.

## Worked example (/example)

The page at https://nuthatch-indexer.com/example is a worked, real-world walkthrough: rebuilding a real
community subgraph — PaulieB14/horizon-indexer-subgraph, credited to Paul Barba's "Horizon
Indexer Performance" subgraph — as a Nuthatch project. It indexes Graph Protocol Horizon
staking activity on Arbitrum One, i.e. The Graph's own network, without using The Graph.

- Contracts and events: HorizonStaking (0x00669A4CF01450B64E8A2A20E9b1FCB71E61eF03,
  OperatorSet); SubgraphService (0xb2Bb92d0DE618878E438b55D5846cfecD9301105; AllocationCreated,
  AllocationClosed, AllocationResized, IndexingRewardsCollected, QueryFeesCollected);
  StakingExtension (0x3bE385576d7C282070Ad91BF94366de9f9ba3571; StakeDelegated,
  StakeDelegatedLocked, StakeDelegatedWithdrawn).
- One `nuthatch init --chain arbitrum-one <three addresses>` fetches ABIs from Sourcify (all
  verified), auto-detects deployment blocks for backfill, and scaffolds a "nest": a directory
  (`nuthatch.toml`, `views/`, `tests/`) that is the unit of indexing definition and can be
  committed, shared, and re-run with `nuthatch init --from <git-url>`.
- Every event becomes a raw table immediately (allocation_created, stake_delegated,
  query_fees_collected, ...), queryable during backfill before any modelling.
- The subgraph's imperative AssemblyScript handlers become declarative incremental views:
  operators (latest OperatorSet per indexer), allocations (Created/Resized/Closed folded to
  current state), delegations (Delegated/Locked/Withdrawn folded per delegator), hourly and
  daily aggregations of rewards/fees/delegations, and global stats as a view over views. On a
  reorg the engine retracts affected rows and downstream views correct themselves; there is no
  hand-written rollback. The global-singleton entity, a subgraph anti-pattern, is free as an
  incremental aggregate.
- `nuthatch test` runs a generated golden suite (fixture blocks in, exact entity states out),
  which is the artefact you review when AI proposes view translations. Migration via
  `nuthatch init --from-subgraph subgraph.yaml` converts manifest and schema mechanically and
  flags proposed handler-to-view translations for human review.
- `nuthatch dev` serves SQL, an auto-generated GraphQL schema shaped to match the original
  subgraph's queries, SSE streams, and the built-in MCP server; an agent pointed at the local
  instance gets schema discovery with nothing leaving the machine.

## Install

- Shell: `curl -fsSL https://nuthatch-indexer.com/install.sh | sh` (verify the script before running).
- Cargo: `cargo install nuthatch`.
- Homebrew: `brew install nuthatch`.
- Docker is only for scaled mode; embedded mode needs no Docker.

## Scope

In scope: EVM chains, embedded and scaled modes, the workflow above. Out of scope: any hosted
service, billing, metering, or multi-tenancy; any token, staking, or decentralized-network
feature; non-EVM chains before EVM is airtight; trusted-execution attestation or
zero-knowledge proofs.

## Links

- Website: https://nuthatch-indexer.com/
- Install: https://nuthatch-indexer.com/install
- Worked example: https://nuthatch-indexer.com/example
- Manifesto: https://nuthatch-indexer.com/manifesto
- Source, docs, discussions: https://github.com/cargopete/nuthatch
- Licence: AGPL-3.0-only
